domain.name: Piazza del Duomo, Milan
domain.description: The heart of Milan, anchored by the Duomo cathedral, surrounded by the Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II shopping arcade to the north and the Palazzo Reale to the south. A UNESCO site and one of Italy's most iconic public squares.

entities.name: Duomo di Milano
entities.shortDescription: The Duomo di Milano (Cathedral of Santa Maria Nascente) is one of the world's largest Gothic cathedrals and the spiritual heart of Milan. Construction began in 1386 and continued for nearly six centuries.

entities.name: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II
entities.shortDescription: One of the world's oldest shopping arcades, designed by Giuseppe Mengoni. Completed in 1877, it forms the northern edge of Piazza del Duomo and is a masterpiece of 19th-century iron and glass architecture.
entities.location: North of Piazza del Duomo, Milan

entities.name: Palazzo Reale
entities.shortDescription: The Royal Palace of Milan, built in the 18th century as the seat of the Habsburg rulers. Today it serves as a cultural venue and museum, located on the south side of Piazza del Duomo.
entities.location: South side of Piazza del Duomo, Milan

entities.name: Piazza del Duomo
entities.shortDescription: The principal plaza of Milan, formed by the Duomo at its center, the Galleria to the north, and the Palazzo Reale to the south. One of the most recognizable squares in Europe.

facts.title: Foundation of the Duomo
facts.body: Construction of the Duomo di Milano began in 1386 under Archbishop Antonio da Saluzzo and continued for nearly six centuries, a span that marked Milan's transition from medieval commune to modern metropolis.

facts.title: Candoglia Marble
facts.body: Quarried at Lake Maggiore and floated in by canal, the distinctive pink-veined Candoglia marble gives the cathedral its characteristic blush tone. This choice reflects Milan's connection to northern Alpine trade routes.

facts.title: Gothic Spires
facts.body: The first gothic spires rise during the medieval period. The full set of 135 spires won't be completed until the 19th century, each topped with a statue or finial.

facts.title: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II Completion
facts.body: Designed by Giuseppe Mengoni and completed between 1865 and 1877, the Galleria is one of the world's oldest shopping arcades. It forms the northern edge of Piazza del Duomo and showcases cutting-edge iron and glass construction techniques.

facts.title: Glass and Iron Dome
facts.body: The iconic glass and iron dome of the Galleria is an engineering marvel of the late 19th century. The octagonal space where four barrel vaults meet creates a breathtaking central gathering point and shopping experience.

facts.title: Palazzo Reale Construction
facts.body: Built in the 18th century as the seat of the Habsburg rulers of Milan, Palazzo Reale represents neoclassical architecture. It served as the official royal residence during the Austrian administration of Lombardy.

facts.title: Piazza Duomo during WWII
facts.body: In August 1943 Allied bombing severely damaged buildings around Piazza Duomo. The cathedral itself was struck but its main structure survived, and the square became a symbolic stage for Milan's 1945 liberation.

facts.title: Over 3,400 Statues
facts.body: The Duomo is adorned with more than 3,400 statues, more than any other building in the world. They populate the spires, parapets, and niches, each telling a story from religious or civic history.

facts.title: The Madonnina Statue
facts.body: Placed at the cathedral spire's tip in 1774, the gilded Madonnina (little Madonna) is Milan's spiritual high point. A long-standing tradition holds that no building in Milan should rise above the Madonnina.

facts.title: Visconti Era
facts.body: Under Duke Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duomo became a symbol of Milanese power and prestige. The Visconti family funded construction generously, aiming to create the largest and most magnificent Gothic cathedral in Italy. Over 3,400 statues were commissioned during this period, and international architects were invited to work on the project, making it a center of European Gothic innovation.

facts.title: Sforza Rule
facts.body: During the Sforza dynasty (1450-1535), Milan flourished as a major Renaissance power. The Sforza family continued generous patronage of the Duomo's construction, while also establishing Milan as a center of art and culture. The Sforza period saw the continuation of the cathedral's elaborate decorative programs and the commissioning of numerous statues and architectural elements.

facts.title: Habsburg Period
facts.body: The Habsburg period (1535-1700s) saw Austrian rule over Milan and Lombardy. During this era, the Palazzo Reale was built in the 18th century as the seat of the Habsburg rulers and served as the official royal residence. This period brought neoclassical architecture and administrative changes to Piazza del Duomo, reshaping the political and cultural landscape of Milan.
